content.text: (keyboard commands) to dramatically improve notebook productivity and efficiency.
are shorthand annotations that change how a cell's text is executed. They provide special functionality beyond regular Python code.

# %time: Time single execution
%time sum(range(1000))  # Output: Wall time: 0.000123 sec

# %timeit: Time multiple executions (best practice)
%timeit sum(range(1000))  # Output: 27.5 µs per loop (100000 loops)

# With function
def my_func(x):
    return x ** 2

%timeit my_func(100)  # Time function execution

# %ls: List files in directory
%ls

# %ls with wildcards
%ls *.txt

# %pwd: Show current working directory
%pwd  # Output: /content

# %cd: Change directory
%cd /content/data

# %mkdir: Create directory
%mkdir new_folder

# %cat: View file contents
%cat file.txt

# %debug: Interactive debugger after error
%debug  # Runs pdb after exception

# %pdb: Enable automatic debugging
%pdb on  # Automatically enter debugger on error

# %who: List variables in namespace
%who

# %whos: Detailed variable info
%whos  # Shows name, type, value

# %matplotlib inline: Show plots in notebook
%matplotlib inline

# %autoreload: Reload modules automatically
%load_ext autoreload
%autoreload 2  # Reload before executing each line

# %load_ext: Load extension
%load_ext memory_profiler

# %reload_ext: Reload extension
%reload_ext autoreload

# %ls: List files (same as !ls)
%ls

# %pwd: Print working directory
%pwd

# %cd: Change directory
%cd /path/to/dir

# %env: Set environment variable
%env MY_VAR=value

# %history: Show command history
%history

# %%time: Time entire cell
%%time
sum(range(1000))
sum(range(2000))
# Output: Executes all code, times total

# %%timeit: Time entire cell multiple times
%%timeit
sum(range(1000))

# %%writefile: Write cell contents to file
%%writefile hello.py
print("Hello, World!")
# Creates hello.py with the code

# %%capture: Capture output to variable
%%capture output
print("This won't show")
print("This won't show either")
# output.text contains captured output

# %%file: Same as %%writefile
%%file notebook.md
# This is markdown content

# %%bash: Run bash commands %%bash echo "Hello from bash" ls -la # %%script python3: Run Python 3 code %%script python3 print("Running in separate Python process") # %%sh: Run shell commands %%sh pwd ls # %%html: Render HTML %%html

Hello World

This is HTML

# %%javascript: Run JavaScript %%javascript console.log("Hello JavaScript")

# %%capture: Suppress output
%%capture
print("This won't display")
result = 42  # Won't show

# %%debug: Interactive debugger
%%debug
x = 10
y = 20
z = x + y  # Debugger pauses here

# %%prun: Profile code
%%prun
sum(range(10000))

# Install packages
!pip install requests numpy

# Download files
!wget https://example.com/data.zip

# Clone git repository
!git clone https://github.com/user/repo.git

# List files
!ls

# Show current directory
!pwd

# Create directory
!mkdir new_folder

# Remove file
!rm file.txt
Run cell and go to next
Run cell and add new below

# Jupyter: Ctrl + A (add cell above)
# Colab: Ctrl + M + A (add cell above)

# Jupyter: Ctrl + B (add cell below)
# Colab: Ctrl + M + B (add cell below)

# Jupyter: Ctrl + Y (change to code)
# Colab: Ctrl + M + Y (change to code)

# Jupyter: Ctrl + M (change to markdown)
# Colab: Ctrl + M + M (change to markdown)

# ❌ Bad: %time only runs once
%time sum(range(1000))

# ✅ Good: %timeit averages multiple runs
%timeit sum(range(1000))

# Reload modules before each execution
%load_ext autoreload
%autoreload 2

# No need to re-import after editing
import my_module
my_module.function()  # Always uses latest version

# ❌ Verbose
print(f"Variable type: {type(x)}")
print(f"Variable value: {x}")

# ✅ Concise
%whos  # Shows all variables with details
Combine Cell Magics for Complex Tasks

# Write executable script
%%writefile analysis.py
%matplotlib inline
import pandas as pd
df = pd.read_csv("data.csv")
print(df.describe())
Mastering Jupyter magics and shortcuts delivers concrete productivity gains in day-to-day AI and data science work:
%timeit automatically runs a function thousands of times and reports mean ± std, giving statistically valid benchmarks with zero setup code.
No kernel restarts to pick up code changes.
%autoreload 2 automatically re-imports modified Python modules before each cell execution, so you can edit a helper module and immediately test it in the notebook.
%matplotlib inline (and %matplotlib widget in JupyterLab) makes matplotlib figures appear directly under the cell that created them.
Shift+Enter / Ctrl+Enter to run, A/B to insert cells, DD to delete — common operations drop from 3–5 clicks to a single keystroke.
Write a Python file directly from a cell, then import it in the next cell — useful for building reusable utility modules during exploration.
Capture verbose !pip install or model-download output into a variable, keeping notebook output clean while still having access to it for debugging.
Code using % or %% magics cannot be run as a plain Python script — it only works inside IPython/Jupyter. This makes it harder to convert notebook code to production scripts.
Each !command runs in a fresh shell, so cd changes made with ! do not persist to the next cell. Use %cd (a magic) for persistent directory changes.
Shortcuts differ between Jupyter and Colab.
Colab requires Ctrl+M prefix for most command-mode shortcuts, making muscle memory non-transferable between the two environments.
Functions that hit the network or disk give misleading results because the first run warms caches, making subsequent runs appear faster.
Writing your own magic requires inheriting from IPython.core.magic.Magics and calling register_magics() — more setup than a plain function.

attrs.alt: Jupyter magic command dispatch architecture showing user input, IPython kernel dispatcher, and three execution paths: built-in magics, extension magics, and shell commands

content.text: When you run a cell, the IPython kernel inspects the first character(s) before executing Python. A single % triggers the line magic path: the rest of the line is parsed as the magic name plus arguments, and the corresponding registered function is called. A double %% triggers cell magic: the entire cell body is passed to the magic function as a string. A ! prefix bypasses the magic system entirely and passes the rest of the line to a subprocess shell via os.system() or subprocess.

Keyboard shortcuts are handled entirely in the browser front-end (JavaScript), not by the Python kernel. This is why Ctrl+Enter runs a cell without restarting the kernel — the browser sends a 'run cell' message to the kernel via WebSocket, then renders the output returned. This separation means shortcuts work even if the kernel is busy computing (though the execution is queued).
Real-world example: AI model benchmarking notebook
Here is a realistic pattern for benchmarking different LLM inference configurations in a Jupyter notebook, using magics and shortcuts throughout:

# Cell 1: Setup — run once, autoreload so edits to utils.py take effect immediately
%load_ext autoreload
%autoreload 2
%matplotlib inline

import utils  # edit utils.py and re-run cells without kernel restart

# Cell 2: Install dependencies silently (keep output clean in shared notebooks)
%%capture install_output
!pip install -q transformers accelerate bitsandbytes
# Access captured output if something fails:
# print(install_output.stdout)

# Cell 3: Check environment
%env  # list all environment variables (inspect available GPU, API keys)

import os
print('CUDA visible:', os.environ.get('CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES', 'all'))
print('Model path:', os.environ.get('MODEL_PATH', './models'))

# Cell 4: Profile forward pass timing across batch sizes
import torch
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, AutoTokenizer

model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained('gpt2').cuda()
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained('gpt2')

def forward_pass(batch_size=1, seq_len=128):
    ids = torch.randint(0, 50257, (batch_size, seq_len)).cuda()
    with torch.no_grad():
        return model(ids)

# Time 10 runs with statistical summary
for bs in [1, 4, 16]:
    print(f'\nbatch_size={bs}')
    %timeit -n 10 -r 3 forward_pass(batch_size=bs)

# Cell 5: Write profiling results to a file for later analysis
%%writefile benchmark_results.py
# Auto-generated benchmark summary
results = {
    'batch_size_1':  '12.3 ms ± 0.4 ms',
    'batch_size_4':  '14.1 ms ± 0.6 ms',
    'batch_size_16': '19.8 ms ± 1.1 ms',
}
print('Peak throughput batch size:', max(results, key=lambda k: int(k.split('_')[-1])))
%time runs code once — a single data point subject to random OS jitter. %timeit runs many iterations and reports mean ± std. Always use %timeit for performance comparisons.
!cd /path runs in a subshell that exits immediately — the next cell still runs in the original directory. Use %cd (magic) to change the notebook's working directory persistently.
Pressing A alone in Colab inserts the letter 'a' in a text field. You need Ctrl+M, A. This trips up Jupyter users switching to Colab.
Using %run on files with relative imports.
%run script.py runs the file in the notebook's namespace, but relative imports (from . import helper) fail because %run doesn't set __package__. Use !python script.py or reorganise imports.
%%capture captures both stdout and stderr — including error messages. If setup fails silently, check capture_var.stderr before assuming everything is fine.

Magic overhead is negligible for all practical purposes. The IPython kernel adds a few microseconds of dispatch overhead per magic invocation, which is immeasurable next to the actual computation. The main performance considerations are:

By default, %timeit automatically calibrates the number of loops. For very slow functions (> 1 second), it may only run 3 times total. Use -n and -r flags to control this explicitly: %timeit -n 5 -r 2 slow_function().
%%prun runs Python's cProfile on the entire cell and prints per-function time. This is the fastest way to identify which function in a data pipeline is the bottleneck.

# Profile an entire pipeline cell to find the bottleneck
%%prun -s cumulative
import pandas as pd
df = pd.read_csv('large_dataset.csv')
df['tokens'] = df['text'].str.split().str.len()
result = df.groupby('label')['tokens'].mean()
print(result)
# Output shows: ncalls, tottime, cumtime per function
# Sort by cumulative time (-s cumulative) to see top-level callers first
!shell commands execute with full user privileges.
A malicious notebook shared via email or download can run arbitrary shell commands when its cells are executed. Never run untrusted notebooks without reviewing them first.
%%writefile /etc/passwd would overwrite the system password file (if the process has permissions). Always use relative paths or explicitly scoped directories.
Colab auto-runs on open can be disabled.
In Google Colab, you can disable 'run code automatically' in settings to prevent notebooks from auto-executing on open — a safer default for shared notebooks.
Keep secrets out of notebook cells.
Use %env VAR=value only for non-sensitive configuration. For API keys, load from environment or dotenv files, and ensure .env files are in .gitignore.
in Generative AI development, enabling rapid experimentation, efficient debugging, and streamlined workflow for ML model development and data analysis.
